## DEEP GATED CANONICAL CORRELATION ANALYSIS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) models can extract informative correlated representations from multimodal unlabelled data. Despite their success, CCA models may break if the number of variables exceeds the number of samples. We propose Deep Gated-CCA, a method for learning correlated representations based on a sparse subset of variables from two observed modalities. The proposed procedure learns two non-linear transformations and simultaneously gates the input variables to identify a subset of most correlated variables. The nonlinear transformations are learned by training two neural networks to maximize a shared correlation loss defined based on their outputs. Gating is obtained by adding an approximate _ℓ_ 0 regularization term applied to the input variables. This approximation relies on a recently proposed continuous Gaussian based relaxation for Bernoulli variables which act as gates. We demonstrate the efficacy of the method using several synthetic and real examples. Most notably, the method outperforms other linear and non-linear CCA models.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) (Hotelling, 1936; Thompson, 2005), is a classic statistical method for finding the maximally correlated linear transformations of two modalities (or views). Using modalities _**X** ∈_ R _[D][x][×][N]_ and _**Y** ∈_ R _[D][Y][ ×][N]_ , which are centered and have _N_ samples with _Dx_ and _Dy_ features respectively. CCA seeks canonical vectors _**ai** ∈_ R _[D][X] ,_ and _**bi** ∈_ R _[D][Y] ,_ such that _,_ _**u** i_ = _**a[T] i[X]**[,]_[and] _**[ v][i]**_ **[=]** _**[b][T] i[Y]**[ , i]_[=][1] _[, ..., N]_[,][maximize][the][sample][correlations] between _**ui**_ and _**vi**_ , where _**ui**_ ( _**vi**_ ) form an orthonormal basis for _i_ = 1 _, ..., d_ , i.e.

While CCA enjoys a closed-form solution using a generalized eigen pair problem, it is restricted to the linear transformations _**A**_ = [ _**a**_ 1 _, ...,_ _**a** d_ ] and _**B**_ = [ _**b**_ 1 _, ...,_ _**b** d_ ].

In order to identify non-linear relations between input variables, several extensions of CCA have been proposed. Kernel methods such as Kernel CCA (Bach & Jordan, 2002), Non-paramatric CCA (Michaeli et al., 2016) or Multi-view Diffusion maps (Lindenbaum et al., 2020) learn the non-linear relations in reproducing Hilbert spaces. These methods have several shortcomings: they are limited to a designed kernel, they require _O_ ( _N_[2] ) computations for training, and they have poor interpolation and extrapolation capabilities. To overcome these limitations, Andrew et al. (2013) have proposed Deep CCA, to learn parametric non-linear transformations of the input modalities _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . The functions are learned by training two neural networks to maximize the total correlation between their outputs.

Linear and non-linear canonical correlation models have been widely used in the setting of unsupervised or semi-supervised learning. When _d_ is set to a dimension satisfying _d < Dx, Dy_ , these models find dimensional reduced representations that may be useful for clustering, classification or manifold learning in many applications. For example, in biology (Pimentel et al., 2018), neuroscience (Al-Shargie et al., 2017), medicine (Zhang et al., 2017), and engineering (Chen et al., 2017). One key limitations of these models is that they typically require more samples than features, i.e. _N > Dx, Dy_ . However, if we have more variable than samples, the estimation based on the closed form solution of the CCA problem (in Eq. 1) breaks (Suo et al., 2017). Moreover, in high dimensional data, often some of the variables are not informative and thus should be omitted

from the transformations. For these reasons, there has been a growing interest in studying sparse CCA models.

Sparse CCA (SCCA) (Waaijenborg et al., 2008; Hardoon & Shawe-Taylor, 2011; Suo et al., 2017) uses an _ℓ_ 1 penalty to encourage sparsity of the canonical vectors _**a** i_ and _**b** i_ . This can not only remove the degeneracy inherit to _N < Dx, Dy_ , but can improve interpetability and performance. One caveat of this approach is its high computational complexity, which can be reduced by replacing the orthonormality constraints on _**u** i_ and _**v** i_ with orthonormality constraints on _**a** i_ and _**b** i_ . This procedure is known as simplified-SCCA (Parkhomenko et al., 2009; Witten et al., 2009), which enjoys a closed form solution. There has been limited work on extending these models to sparse nonlinear CCA. Specifically, there are two kernel based extensions, two-stage kernel CCA (TSKCCA) by Yoshida et al. (2017) and SCCA based on Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (SCCAHSIC) by Uurtio et al. (2018). However, these models suffer from the same limitations as KCCA and are not scalable to a high dimensional regime.

This paper presents a sparse CCA model that can be optimized using standard deep learning methodologies. The method combines the differentiable loss presented in DCCA (Andrew et al., 2013) with an approximate _ℓ_ 0 regularization term designed to sparsify the input variables of both _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . Our regularization relies on a recently proposed Gaussian based continuous relaxation of Bernoulli random variables, termed gates (Yamada et al., 2020). The gates are applied to the input features to sparsify _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . The gates parameters are trained jointly via stochastic gradient decent to maximize the correlation between the representations of _**X**_ and _**Y**_ , while simultaneously selecting only the subsets of the most correlated input features. We apply the proposed method to synthetic data, and demonstrate that our method can improve the estimation of the canonical vectors compared with SCCA models. Then, we use the method to identify informative variable in multichannel noisy seismic data and show its advantage over other CCA models.

## 1.4 STOCHASTIC GATES

In the last few years, several methods have been proposed for incorporating discrete random variables into gradient based optimization methods. Towards this goal, continuous relaxations of discrete random variables such as (Maddison et al., 2016; Jang et al., 2017) have been proposed.

Such relaxations have been used in several applications, for example, model compression (Louizos et al., 2017), feature selection or for defining discrete activations (Jang et al., 2016). We focus on a Gaussian-based relaxation of Bernoulli variables, termed Stochastic Gates (STG) (Yamada et al., 2020), which were originally proposed for supervised feature selection. ## 2 DEEP GATED CCA

## 2.1 MODEL

It is appealing to try to combine ideas from Sparse CCA into the rich differentiable model of Deep CCA. However, a straight forward _ℓ_ 1 regularization of the input layer of a neural network does not work in practice because it makes the learning procedure unstable. This was observed in the supervised setting by Li et al. (2016); Feng & Simon (2017). This instability occurs because the objective is not differentiable everywhere. To overcome this limitation, we use the STG random variables (see Eq. 3) by multiplying them with the features of _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . Then, by penalizing for active gates using a regularization term E _∥_ **z** _∥_ 0, we can induce sparsity in the input variables.

We formulate the problem of sparse nonlinear CCA by regularizing a deep neural network with a correlation term. We introduce two random STG vectors into the input layers of two neural networks which are trained in tandem to maximize the total correlation. Denoting the random gating vectors **z** _x_ and **z** _y_ for view _**X**_ and _**Y**_ respectively, the Deep Gated CCA (DG-CCA) loss is defined by

where _**θ**_ = ( _**θX** ,_ _**θY**_ ), _**µ**_ = ( _**µX** ,_ _**µY**_ ) are the model parameters, and _λx_ , _λy_ are regularization parameters that control the sparsity of the input variables. The vectors **z** _x_ and **z** _y_ are random STG vectors, with elements defined based on Eq. 3.

Fig. 2 highlights the proposed architecture. Each observed modality is first passed through the gates. Then, the outputs of the gates are used as inputs to a view-specific neural sub-net. Finally, the shared loss term in Eq. 4 is minimized by optimizing the parameters of the gates and neural sub-nets.

## 3 RESULTS

In the following section we detail the evaluation of the proposed approach using synthetic and real datasets. We start with two linear examples, demonstrating the performance of DG-CCA when _N ≪ DX , DY_ . Then, we use noisy images from MNIST and seismic data measured using two channels to demonstrate that DG-CCA finds meaningful representations of data even in a noisy regime. For a full description of the training procedure as well as the baseline methods, we refer the reader to the Appendix.

## 3.2 MULTI VIEW SPINNING PUPPETS

As an illustrative example we use a dataset collected by Lederman & Talmon (2018) for multiview learning. The authors have generated two videos capturing rotations of 3 desk puppets. One camera captures two puppets, while the other captures another two, where one puppet is shared across cameras. A snapshot from both cameras appears in the top row of Fig. 4. All puppets are placed on a spinning device that rotates the dolls at different frequencies. In both video there is a shared

parameter, namely the rotation of the common bulldog. Even thought the Bulldog is captured from a slightly different angle, we attempt to use CCA to identify a linear transformation that projects the two Bulldogs in to a common embedding. We use a subset of the spinning puppets dataset, with 400 images from each camera. Each image has 240 _×_ 320 = 76800 pixels (using a gray scaled version of the colored image), therefore, there are more feature than samples and direct application of CCA would fail. We apply the proposed scheme using _λy_ = _λx_ = 50, a linear activation and embedding dimension _d_ = 2. DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . The active gates are presented in the bottom row of Fig. 4.

In Fig. 5 we present the coupled two dimensional embedding of both videos. Both embeddings are highly correlated with the angular orientation of the Bulldog. Note that adjacent images in the embedding are not necessarily adjacent in the original ambient space, this is because the Bunny and the Yoda puppets are gated and do not affect the embedding.

**Figure 4:** Top: two samples from the spinning puppets videos. Arrows indicate the spinning direction of each puppet. Bottom: the converged active gates for each video. There are 372 and 403 active gates for the left and right videos respectively.

## 3.3 NOISY MNIST

MNIST (LeCun et al., 2010) which consists of 28 _×_ 28 grayscale digit images, with 60K/10K images for training/testing, is a well known and widely investigated dataset. We use a challenging variant of MNIST with coupled noisy views. The first view is created by adding noise drawn uniformly from [0 _,_ 1] to all pixels. The second view is created by placing a random patch from a natural image in the background of the handwritten digits. Both views are based on different digit instances drawn from the same digit class. Random samples from both views are presented in Fig. 6. Both views consists of 62 _,_ 000 samples, of which we use 40 _,_ 000 for training 12 _,_ 000 for testing and 10 _,_ 000 are used as a validation set. Here the validation set is used for early stopping of the training procedure and optimizing _λ_ = _λx_ = _λy_ .

Multi-view processing of the two noisy views can generate an informative representation of the noisy MNIST data. In the following we will focus on performing unsupervised embedding of each noisy MNIST view into correlated 10 dimensional space.

By reducing the correlation cost, the DG-CCA learns which per-view pixels are relevant and informative in correlation maximization sense. In the bottom right corner of Fig 6 we present the location of the active gates. DG-CCA selects features (pixels) within an oval-like shape in the center of each view thus capturing the digit information and reducing the influence of the noise.

To measure the class separation in the learned embedding, we apply _k_ -means clustering to the stacked embedding of the two views. We run _k_ -means (with _k_ = 10) using 20 random initializations and record the run with the smallest sum of square distances from centroids. Given the cluster assignment, _k_ -means clustering accuracy (KM ACC) and mutual information (MI) are measured using the true labels. Additionally, we train a Linear-SVM (LSVM) model on our train and validation datasets. LSVM classification accuracy (LSVM ACC) is measured on the remaining test set. The performance of DG-CCA compared with several baselines appears in Table 2. In the appendix we provide all implementation details and provide an experiment demonstrating the performance for various values of _λ_ = _λx_ = _λy_ .

## 3.4 SEISMIC EVENT CLASSIFICATION

Next, we evaluate the method using a dataset studied by Lindenbaum et al. (2018). The data consists of 1609 seismic events. Here, we focus on 537 explosions which are categorized into 3 quarries. The events occurred between the years 2004 _−_ 2015, in the southern region of Israel and Jordan. Each event is recorded using two directional channels facing east (E) and north (N), these comprise the coupled views for the correlation analysis. Following the analysis by Lindenbaum et al. (2018), the input features are Sonogram representations of the seismic signal. Sonograms are time frequency

## 4 CONCLUSION

In this paper we present a method for learning sparse non-linear transformations which maximize the canonical correlations between two modalities. Our method is realized by gating the input layers of two neural networks which are trained to maximize their output’s total correlations. Input variables are gated using a regularization term which encourages sparsity. This allows us to learn informative representations even when the number of variables far exceeds the number of samples. Finally, we demonstrate that the method outperforms existing methods for linear and non-linear canonical correlation analysis.

## A APPENDIX

## A.1 GATES INITIALIZATION

The Gaussian based stochastic gates suggested by Yamada et al. (2020) are based on trainable parameters _**µ**_ and a constant parameter _σ_ . These control the mean and standard deviation of the injected noise respectively. Yamada et al. progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
**High-Level Feedback:**

This paper addresses a well-motivated problem—extending Canonical Correlation Analysis to simultaneously learn nonlinear representations and perform feature selection in the high-dimensional regime (N < D_x, D_y)—by combining Deep CCA with stochastic gates based on a Gaussian-relaxed ℓ₀ penalty. The proposed approach is a sensible and natural synthesis of two existing ideas (DCCA by Andrew et al., 2013 and stochastic gates by Yamada et al., 2020), and the problem setting is relevant to numerous scientific applications. However, based on the introduction, the paper's **technical novelty appears incremental**: the contribution largely amounts to applying off-the-shelf stochastic gates to the inputs of an existing DCCA architecture, and the introduction does not clearly articulate what non-trivial methodological or theoretical challenges had to be overcome beyond this combination. The **clarity of exposition is generally adequate** but suffers from several issues: key equations referenced in the text (e.g., the CCA objective and the DCCA/SCCA objectives) are mentioned but not displayed in the excerpt, making the formulation harder to follow; there are minor grammatical and typographical problems (e.g., "non-paramatric," "interpetability," "gradient decent," "inherit to"); and the related work on sparse nonlinear CCA (TSKCCA, SCCA-HSIC) is dismissed very briefly without substantive comparison. Most significantly, the introduction promises only synthetic experiments and a single seismic real-data example, which raises concerns about whether the **empirical validation is sufficiently broad and rigorous** to support the strong claim of outperforming other linear and nonlinear CCA models—ideally, the paper should include multiple real-world high-dimensional datasets (e.g., genomics, multi-view imaging), comparisons against TSKCCA/SCCA-HSIC and modern deep sparse baselines, ablations isolating the contribution of the gates versus the DCCA backbone, and analysis of stability and feature-selection consistency. Addressing these points would substantially strengthen the paper's contribution.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in definition of X
comments.quote: Using modalities _**X** ∈_ R _[D][x][×][N]_ and _**Y** ∈_ R _[D][Y][ ×][N]_
comments.explanation: The subscripts in the dimensions are inconsistent: X is defined with dimension D_x × N (lowercase x), while later in the same sentence the canonical vector a_i is defined as being in R^{D_X} (uppercase X). Throughout the paper both D_x and D_X appear to refer to the same quantity (similarly D_y vs D_Y). While a careful reader will immediately recognize these as the same, the inconsistency in subscript capitalization is present in the formal definitions. This is a minor notational inconsistency rather than a substantive error.

comments.title: Typo in sparse CCA constraint description
comments.quote: however, if _∥_ _**a[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1 or _∥_ _**b[T] X** ∥_ 2 _<_ 1, then the covariance in the objective is no longer equal to the correlation.
comments.explanation: The second norm should be ||b^T Y||_2, not ||b^T X||_2, since b is the canonical vector associated with view Y. This appears to be a typo. The meaning is clear from context — the two norm constraints correspond symmetrically to the two views — but as written the constraint mentions X twice.

comments.title: Correlation value of 1.99 exceeds maximum possible
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ .
comments.explanation: Initially this looked odd because correlation is bounded by 1. However, given that the embedding dimension is d=2, the 'total correlation' being reported is likely the sum of correlations across the two canonical components (maximum 2). This is consistent with DCCA convention where the loss is the sum of canonical correlations. Still, the paper refers to it as 'a correlation of 1.99' without clarifying that this is the sum across d=2 components, which could mislead a careful reader who expects a single correlation value ≤ 1. A brief clarification would help.

comments.title: Post-training gate thresholding criterion
comments.quote: After training we remove the stochastic part of the gates, and use only variables _ix ∈{_ 1 _, ..., DX }_ and _iy ∈{_ 1 _, ..., Dy}_ such that z _x_ [ _ix_ ] _>_ 0 and z _y_ [ _iy_ ] _>_ 0.
comments.explanation: The stochastic gate is defined as max(0, min(1, μ + ε)) with ε ~ N(0, σ²). After training, removing the stochastic part means setting ε=0, so the gate value becomes max(0, min(1, μ)). The criterion 'z[i] > 0' then reduces to μ[i] > 0, which means any feature with even a tiny positive μ is kept. This is an unusual threshold — in the original STG paper (Yamada et al. 2020), features are typically selected when μ > 0.5, since μ = 0.5 corresponds to a 'fair' Bernoulli (as the paper itself states in Fig. 1 description). Using μ > 0 as the threshold would retain many features whose expected gate value is close to 0. This is either a notation ambiguity or an unusual choice worth clarifying, as it affects reproducibility and the 'sparsity' claim.

comments.title: Table 1 caption uses ψ but body uses φ
comments.quote: **Table 1:** Evaluating the estimation quality of the canonical vectors _**ψ**_ and _**η**_ .
comments.explanation: The table caption refers to canonical vectors ψ and η, but throughout the surrounding text (and in the generative model definition Σ_XY = ρ Σ_X (φη^T) Σ_Y) the canonical vectors are φ and η. The column header E_φ in the same table confirms the intended symbol is φ. This is a minor inconsistency (likely a typo, ψ vs φ) that could briefly confuse readers but is easily resolved from context.

comments.title: Formula for empirical correlation ρ̂ appears malformed
comments.quote: which is also _ρ_ = _**φXY**_ **[ˆ]** _[T]_ _**η**[T]_
comments.explanation: I initially tried to parse this as ρ̂ = φ̂^T X Y^T η̂ (which would make sense as the sample cross-correlation between the two canonical variates φ̂^T X and η̂^T Y, up to a normalization factor of 1/(N-1)). The figure caption confirms this intended form: 'ρ̂ = φ̂^T X^T Y η̂'. Note however that the figure caption version itself has a transpose problem: if X ∈ R^{D_x × N} (as defined), then φ̂^T X ∈ R^{1 × N}, and to get a scalar correlation we need φ̂^T X Y^T η̂ (not φ̂^T X^T Y η̂). So both expressions appear to have inconsistent transposes given X ∈ R^{D_x × N}.

comments.title: Estimation error formula may have incorrect form
comments.quote: we evaluate the estimation error of _**φ**_ using _E_ _**φ**_ = 2(1 _−|_ _**φ**[T]_[ ˆ] _**φ** |_ )
comments.explanation: The squared distance between two unit vectors φ and φ̂ (accounting for sign ambiguity) is ||φ - sign(φ^T φ̂)φ̂||^2 = 2(1 - |φ^T φ̂|). So the formula here is the squared distance, not the distance. The authors may simply be calling the squared deviation an 'estimation error', which is a defensible but nonstandard choice. Since they define what they use explicitly, this is not an error per se, just worth noting that it is not the Euclidean distance between unit vectors.

comments.title: Table 1 column header says E_η but text says error of ρ
comments.quote: In Table 1 we present the estimated correlation along with the estimation errors of _**φ**_ and _**ρ**_ (averaged over 100 simulations).
comments.explanation: The text says Table 1 reports estimation errors of φ and ρ, but Table 1 actually shows columns labeled ρ̂, E_φ, and E_η (the estimation error for the second canonical vector η, not ρ). The 'ρ' in the prose is almost certainly a typo for 'η'. This is a minor mismatch between prose and table but does not affect any claim.

comments.title: Total correlation formula appears to have an asymmetric exponent
comments.explanation: The standard formula for total correlation in DCCA uses symmetric −1/2 exponents on both Σ̂_X and Σ̂_Y, giving trace((T^T T)^{1/2}) with T = Σ̂_X^{−1/2} Σ̂_{XY} Σ̂_Y^{−1/2}. The form shown in the paper (−1/2 on Σ̂_X but −1 on Σ̂_Y) is nonstandard; by the cyclic trace identity it equals trace of Σ̂_X^{−1} Σ̂_{XY} Σ̂_Y^{−1} Σ̂_{YX}, which is the squared Frobenius-type quantity rather than the sum of canonical correlations. The text also lacks the outer square root / trace-norm that would give the sum of singular values. Inconsistency with the standard DCCA loss (Andrew et al. 2013) is worth noting.

comments.title: Initialization procedure references undefined thresholded vector
comments.quote: The initial values of the parameters of the gates are then defined by _**µ** X_ = _**u**_ **¯** + 0 _._ 5, and _**µ** Y_ = _**v**_ **¯** + 0 _._ 5, where _**u**_ **¯** and _**u**_ **¯** are the thresholded versions of the absolute value of the singular vectors.
comments.explanation: The text defines μ_Y in terms of v̄, but then says 'where ū and ū are the thresholded versions' — the second ū should clearly be v̄. This creates ambiguity because the reader must infer that v̄ is the thresholded absolute value of v. Combined with the fact that 'thresholded' here presumably means set-to-zero-below-threshold while keeping absolute values (since we add 0.5 to get μ values), this initialization procedure becomes unclear: if thresholded values below δ are set to 0, then μ = 0.5 for those, which means they still start as 'fair' gates and not shifted toward closed. This seems to defeat the 'smarter initialization' purpose or at least should be clarified. Also, the thresholding of u,v uses 'the same percentile used for S_XY' which may not be appropriate since u,v live in different spaces than C_XY entries.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—feature selection for nonlinear CCA in high-dimensional, low-sample multimodal settings—and the proposed combination of DCCA with stochastic gates/approximate \( \ell_0 \) regularization is plausible and potentially useful. However, based on the introduction, the paper’s clarity and polish are significant concerns: the writing contains many grammatical errors, typos, inconsistent notation, and formatting problems, and several equations appear incomplete or poorly integrated into the text, making the technical setup difficult to follow. The motivation is generally sound, but the novelty relative to existing sparse nonlinear CCA, stochastic-gate feature selection, and regularized DCCA methods needs to be articulated more sharply; at present, the method reads largely as a straightforward combination of known components. The paper should also more carefully distinguish feature-level gating from sparsity of canonical vectors, explain how the stochastic gates interact with the DCCA correlation objective and covariance estimation in minibatches, and discuss whether the approach truly resolves the \(N < D\) degeneracy or merely regularizes it empirically. Claims such as outperforming other linear and nonlinear CCA models are too strong without seeing rigorous evaluation details, ablations, and fair baselines. Overall, the idea has merit, but the presentation requires substantial refinement, stronger theoretical or methodological justification, and clearer positioning to be convincing at a top-tier venue.

comments.title: Sample-size requirement is stated too broadly for all CCA variants discussed
comments.quote: One key limitations of these models is that they typically require more samples than features, i.e. _N > Dx, Dy_ . However, if we have more variable than samples, the estimation based on the closed form solution of the CCA problem (in Eq. 1) breaks (Suo et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The motivation is sound for unregularized linear CCA: empirical within-view covariance matrices become singular when the number of variables exceeds the number of samples, so the classical closed-form generalized eigenvalue solution is ill-posed without regularization or dimensionality reduction. The second sentence captures that standard issue. However, the broader first sentence refers to “linear and non-linear canonical correlation models” generally. Kernel CCA and DCCA have their own computational and statistical issues, but they do not require N>D_x,D_y in the same literal raw-feature-count sense. The claim would be more accurate if restricted to classical unregularized linear CCA, or phrased as a covariance-estimation/overfitting difficulty rather than a general requirement for the whole class.

comments.title: Simplified sparse CCA is described as having a closed-form solution
comments.quote: One caveat of this approach is its high computational complexity, which can be reduced by replacing the orthonormality constraints on _**u** i_ and _**v** i_ with orthonormality constraints on _**a** i_ and _**b** i_ . This procedure is known as simplified-SCCA (Parkhomenko et al., 2009; Witten et al., 2009), which enjoys a closed form solution.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is reasonable: simplified SCCA/PMD-style formulations replace difficult covariance-induced constraints with simpler constraints on the weight vectors, making alternating optimization cheaper. However, saying simplified SCCA “enjoys a closed form solution” is stronger than what is standard for these methods. In Witten et al.-style sparse CCA, each block update can often be expressed via soft-thresholding and normalization when the other vector is fixed, but the joint sparse problem is still nonconvex or bi-convex and is normally solved iteratively rather than by a single global closed-form solution analogous to classical CCA. If the authors mean “closed-form coordinate/block updates,” that should be stated.

comments.title: Claims of outperforming existing CCA methods overstate the scope of the experiments
comments.quote: Most notably, the method outperforms other linear and non-linear CCA models. [...] Finally, we demonstrate that the method outperforms existing methods for linear and non-linear canonical correlation analysis.
comments.explanation: The paper evaluates DG-CCA on selected synthetic and real examples and compares against several CCA-family baselines, so the authors have support for claiming improved empirical performance in those experiments. However, the wording in both the abstract and conclusion reads as a broad claim over existing linear and nonlinear CCA methods in general. The experiments do not constitute an exhaustive comparison against all relevant linear, kernel, regularized, sparse, and deep CCA variants across all regimes. A more defensible phrasing would be that DG-CCA outperforms the evaluated baselines on the reported benchmarks.

comments.title: Total-correlation expression appears to omit the matrix square root / trace norm
comments.quote: The total correlation in Eq. 4 can be expressed using the _−_ 1 _/_ 2  _−_ 1 _−_ 1 _/_ 2 trace of **Σ**[] _Y_ **Σ** _Y X_  **Σ** _X_ **[Σ]**[] _[XY]_ **[Σ]**[] _Y_ .
comments.explanation: The intended covariance definitions appear to match the standard DCCA derivation. In DCCA, the total correlation is the sum of singular values of T=\hatΣ_X^{-1/2}\hatΣ_{XY}\hatΣ_Y^{-1/2}, equivalently tr((T^T T)^{1/2}). If the paper literally uses the trace of \hatΣ_Y^{-1/2}\hatΣ_{YX}\hatΣ_X^{-1}\hatΣ_{XY}\hatΣ_Y^{-1/2} without the outer matrix square root, then it is optimizing the sum of squared canonical correlations rather than the usual DCCA total correlation. This may be a typesetting or OCR loss of the square-root notation, but as written the expression is not equivalent to the stated objective.

comments.title: Post-training gate selection rule is underspecified because the gates are random variables
comments.quote: After training we remove the stochastic part of the gates, and use only variables _ix ∈{_ 1 _, ..., DX }_ and _iy ∈{_ 1 _, ..., Dy}_ such that z _x_ [ _ix_ ] _>_ 0 and z _y_ [ _iy_ ] _>_ 0.
comments.explanation: The intended idea is clear: train with stochastic relaxed gates, then form a deterministic selected feature set. What remains ambiguous is what exactly z denotes after the stochastic part is removed. Earlier, z_i is a random clipped Gaussian variable, while the regularizer uses E||z||_0. If the deterministic gate is max(0,min(1,μ_i)), then the criterion is effectively μ_i>0; if it is based on expected gate value or on P(z_i>0), the selected support could differ. This matters for reproducing the reported number of active gates and for interpreting feature selection. The method section should explicitly state the deterministic test-time gate and threshold.

comments.title: How DG-CCA produces estimated canonical vectors is not defined
comments.quote: Using this model we generate 400 samples and estimate the canonical vectors based on CCA and DG-CCA. [...] Next, we present the values of _**φ**_ , the DG-CCA estimate (using _λ_ = 30) of the canonical vector _**φ**_ **[ˆ]** , and the CCA based estimate of the canonical vector _**a**_ **ˆ** .
comments.explanation: For ordinary linear CCA, an estimated canonical vector is a standard object. DG-CCA, as described, learns stochastic input gates and neural-network transformations; it does not directly define canonical vectors in the same way linear CCA or SCCA does. In the synthetic linear experiment, the authors may be using a one-dimensional linear network and recovering a vector from the product of deterministic gates and linear weights, followed by normalization and sign alignment. But this recovery step is not stated. Different plausible choices—using the gate vector alone, first-layer weights, gate-weight products, or refitting CCA on the selected variables—would yield different estimates and different values of the reported error E_φ. The mapping from a trained DG-CCA model to \hatφ and \hatη needs to be explicit for the comparison to be interpretable and reproducible.

comments.title: Ordinary CCA baselines are not well-defined in high-dimensional settings
comments.quote: Using this model we generate 400 samples and estimate the canonical vectors based on CCA and DG-CCA. [...] Each Sonogram _**z** ∈_ R[1157] with 89 time bins and 13 frequency bins.
comments.explanation: The paper uses ordinary CCA baselines in regimes where the number of features exceeds or is comparable to the number of samples. In the synthetic example there are 800 variables per view and 400 samples; in the seismic experiment each view has 1157 sonogram features while the subset contains 537 explosions. Earlier, the paper correctly notes that standard closed-form CCA breaks or becomes degenerate when within-view covariance matrices are singular. Therefore, the CCA baseline is not unique unless the implementation choice is specified, such as pseudoinverse, ridge regularization, PCA preprocessing, truncation, or a software default. Without this detail, the CCA rows/plots are difficult to reproduce and may reflect a particular numerical convention rather than an intrinsic baseline.

comments.title: The explanation for why ℓ1 input regularization fails is overstated
comments.quote: However, a straight forward _ℓ_ 1 regularization of the input layer of a neural network does not work in practice because it makes the learning procedure unstable. This was observed in the supervised setting by Li et al. (2016); Feng & Simon (2017). This instability occurs because the objective is not differentiable everywhere.
comments.explanation: The motivation for using stochastic gates rather than naive sparsity penalties is understandable, and prior supervised feature-selection work did report practical difficulties with simple ℓ1-style neural feature selection. However, non-differentiability of ℓ1 at zero is not by itself a sufficient explanation for why optimization “does not work”; subgradient, proximal, and smooth-approximation methods are standard. The practical instability may instead arise from scale invariances across network layers, shrinkage bias, interactions among feature weights, or optimization dynamics. As written, the claim overstates a general mathematical obstacle.

comments.title: Empirical correlation formula appears to omit Pearson normalization
comments.quote: **Figure 3:** Left: Regularization path of DG-CCA on data generated from the linear model. The left _y_ -axis (green) represents the sum of active gates (by expectation) after training. The right _y_ -axis represents the empirical correlation between the estimated representations, i.e. _ρ_ ˆ = _**φ**_ **[ˆ]** _T_ _**X** T_ _**Y η**_ **ˆ** , where _**φ**_ **ˆ** and _**η**_ **ˆ** are the estimated canonical vectors.
comments.explanation: The authors appear to intend the sample correlation between the one-dimensional projected views. Even reading the displayed matrix ordering charitably as an OCR/notation artifact, the expression shown is an unnormalized cross-covariance or inner product, not an empirical Pearson correlation, unless additional normalization constraints are imposed on the projected variables. The empirical correlation should divide by the empirical standard deviations of \hatφ^T X and \hatη^T Y. This distinction matters in the high-dimensional setting, where estimated directions need not have unit sample variance. If the plotted quantity is actually the normalized correlation computed by the DCCA loss, the caption should state that rather than giving the unnormalized bilinear form.

comments.title: Evaluation phrase “stacked embedding of the two views” is ambiguous
comments.quote: To measure the class separation in the learned embedding, we apply _k_ -means clustering to the stacked embedding of the two views.
comments.explanation: The evaluation goal is clear: assess whether the learned unsupervised representations separate MNIST digit classes. However, “stacked embedding of the two views” could mean vertically stacking the two sets of view embeddings as 2N embedded samples, or concatenating paired view embeddings into one higher-dimensional representation per sample. Because the two noisy MNIST views are described as different digit instances from the same class, this choice changes the number of clustered points, the feature dimension, and potentially the reported MI/k-means accuracy. The subsequent LSVM description has a similar ambiguity about whether training is done on each view separately, on vertically pooled embeddings, or on concatenated paired embeddings. The evaluation protocol should be specified more precisely.

comments.title: Seismic evaluation protocol lacks a clearly separated test set
comments.quote: We omit 20% of the data as a validation set. Then we train DG-CCA to embed the data in 3 dimensions using several values for _λ_ = _λx_ = _λy_ . Then, we use the model that attains maximal correlation on the validation set. In Table 2 we present the MI, _k_ -means and SVM accuracies computed based on DG-CCA embedding.
comments.explanation: For the seismic data, the only held-out subset mentioned is a validation set, and that same validation set is used to select the regularization parameter by maximal correlation. It is therefore unclear whether MI, k-means accuracy, and SVM accuracy in Table 2 are reported on the training data, on the validation data used for model selection, or on a separate test split not described here. This matters especially for the supervised SVM metric, and using the validation set both for hyperparameter selection and final reporting would bias performance upward. The evaluation split should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: Noise-generation procedure for seismic sonograms is underspecified
comments.quote: We create the noisy seismic data by adding sonograms computed based on vehicle noise from[1] .
comments.explanation: The intent is to make the seismic classification problem harder by contaminating explosion sonograms with vehicle-noise sonograms. However, the exact construction is important and is not specified. It is unclear whether vehicle noise is added in the raw waveform domain or after computing sonograms, how noise segments are selected, whether the same or independent noise is added to the E and N channels, and what amplitude normalization or SNR is used. Since sonograms may be magnitude, power, or log-scaled representations, simply saying that sonograms are added is not enough to reproduce the experiment or judge the corruption severity.

comments.title: Initialization is described as using the CCA closed-form solution, but the stated procedure is an SVD of cross-covariance
comments.quote: we can utilize the closed form solution of the CCA problem to derive a smarter initialization procedure for the parameters of the gates. Specifically, given the empirical covariance matrix _**C** XY_ = _**[XY]**_ ( _N −_ 1) _**[T]**_[, we denote the thresholded covariance matrix by] _**[ S]**[XY]_[ ,]
comments.explanation: Assuming the intended C_XY definition is the usual empirical cross-covariance, the described initialization thresholds C_XY and then takes leading singular vectors of S_XY. Standard CCA does not take leading singular vectors of the raw cross-covariance matrix; it uses a whitened cross-covariance such as Σ_X^{-1/2}Σ_XYΣ_Y^{-1/2}, or an equivalent generalized eigenproblem involving the within-view covariance matrices. The described procedure is closer to a covariance-maximizing/PLS-type initialization or simplified sparse CCA heuristic. It may be a reasonable warm start, especially with standardized data, but calling it the closed-form CCA solution is mathematically misleading outside special cases such as identity within-view covariance.

comments.title: Threshold parameter for gate initialization is not specified reproducibly
comments.quote: Where _δ_ is a threshold value selected based on the estimated sparsity of _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . Specifically, if we assume that _r_ percent of the values should be zeroed, then _δ_ is set to be the _r_ -th percentile of _|_ ( _**C** XY_ ) _|_ . Then we compute the leading singular vectors _**u**_ and _**v**_ of _**S** XY_ . We further threshold the absolute values of these vectors (using the same percentile used for _**S** XY_ .
comments.explanation: The initialization depends on the assumed sparsity level r, but the passage does not state how r is chosen in the experiments or estimated from data. This matters because DG-CCA is a nonconvex neural optimization problem and gate initialization can affect the sparse solution reached. There is also ambiguity in applying the “same percentile” first to all entries of a D_x×D_y matrix and then to entries of the left and right singular vectors: it is unclear whether the percentile is recomputed separately for u and v, whether the same numerical threshold is reused, or whether view-specific sparsity assumptions are allowed. Additional details are needed for reproducibility.

comments.title: Calling μ=0.5 a “fair Bernoulli” initialization is misleading for active-gate probability
comments.quote: Yamada et al. (2020) have initialize all values of _**µ**_ to 0 _._ 5, in this case, the gates approximate “fair” Bernoulli parameters.
comments.explanation: For the clipped Gaussian gate z=max{0,min{1,μ+ε}}, setting μ=0.5 makes the distribution centered around 0.5 and may make the expected gate value roughly resemble a relaxed fair Bernoulli. However, the paper’s sparsity penalty is E||z||_0=Σ_i P(z_i>0)=Σ_i Φ(μ_i/σ). With the common choice σ=0.5, initializing μ=0.5 gives P(z_i>0)=Φ(1)≈0.84, not 0.5. Thus, if “fair Bernoulli” is read as the probability of an active gate, it conflicts with the paper’s own ℓ0 interpretation. The text should clarify whether “fair” refers to E[z_i] rather than P(z_i>0).

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—feature selection for nonlinear CCA in high-dimensional, low-sample multimodal settings—and the proposed combination of DCCA with stochastic gates/approximate \( \ell_0 \) regularization is plausible and potentially useful. However, based on the introduction, the paper’s clarity and polish are significant concerns: the writing contains many grammatical errors, typos, inconsistent notation, and formatting problems, and several equations appear incomplete or poorly integrated into the text, making the technical setup difficult to follow. The motivation is generally sound, but the novelty relative to existing sparse nonlinear CCA, stochastic-gate feature selection, and regularized DCCA methods needs to be articulated more sharply; at present, the method reads largely as a straightforward combination of known components. The paper should also more carefully distinguish feature-level gating from sparsity of canonical vectors, explain how the stochastic gates interact with the DCCA correlation objective and covariance estimation in minibatches, and discuss whether the approach truly resolves the \(N < D\) degeneracy or merely regularizes it empirically. Claims such as outperforming other linear and nonlinear CCA models are too strong without seeing rigorous evaluation details, ablations, and fair baselines. Overall, the idea has merit, but the presentation requires substantial refinement, stronger theoretical or methodological justification, and clearer positioning to be convincing at a top-tier venue.

comments.title: CCA indexing and orthonormality description is imprecise
comments.quote: CCA seeks canonical vectors _**ai** ∈_ R _[D][X] ,_ and _**bi** ∈_ R _[D][Y] ,_ such that _,_ _**u** i_ = _**a[T] i[X]**[,]_[and] _**[ v][i]**_ **[=]** _**[b][T] i[Y]**[ , i]_[=][1] _[, ..., N]_[,][maximize][the][sample][correlations] between _**ui**_ and _**vi**_ , where _**ui**_ ( _**vi**_ ) form an orthonormal basis for _i_ = 1 _, ..., d_ , i.e.
comments.explanation: I infer the authors intend the standard CCA setup: for each canonical component i=1,...,d, u_i=a_i^T X and v_i=b_i^T Y are length-N canonical variate vectors, constrained to have unit empirical variance and to be mutually uncorrelated within each view. The surrounding text later defines A=[a_1,...,a_d] and B=[b_1,...,b_d], so the apparent 'i=1,...,N' may be an OCR or typesetting corruption and is probably meant to be i=1,...,d. Still, as written, the index range conflicts with the later d-dimensional formulation. Also, saying that u_i and v_i 'form an orthonormal basis' is not quite the usual CCA condition unless d=N; normally they form orthonormal/uncorrelated sets under the empirical inner product, not a full basis of the sample space. This is likely easy to fix by stating the standard constraints explicitly, but the current prose could mislead a reader about the dimensional indexing and constraints.

comments.title: Sample-size requirement is stated too broadly for all CCA variants discussed
comments.quote: One key limitations of these models is that they typically require more samples than features, i.e. _N > Dx, Dy_ . However, if we have more variable than samples, the estimation based on the closed form solution of the CCA problem (in Eq. 1) breaks (Suo et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The authors' main motivation is sound for unregularized linear CCA: empirical within-view covariance matrices become singular when the number of variables exceeds the number of samples, so the classical closed-form generalized eigenvalue solution is ill-posed without regularization or dimensionality reduction. The second sentence captures that standard issue. What remains problematic is the broader first sentence, which refers back to 'linear and non-linear canonical correlation models' generally. Kernel CCA has computational/statistical issues of its own but does not require N>D_x,D_y in the same literal feature-count sense, and DCCA optimizes a correlation loss on low-dimensional network outputs, often with covariance regularization, so it is not automatically invalid merely because the raw input dimension exceeds N. The claim would be more accurate if restricted to classical unregularized linear CCA, or phrased as a statistical/overfitting and covariance-estimation difficulty rather than a general requirement for the whole class.

comments.title: Simplified sparse CCA is described as having a closed-form solution
comments.quote: One caveat of this approach is its high computational complexity, which can be reduced by replacing the orthonormality constraints on _**u** i_ and _**v** i_ with orthonormality constraints on _**a** i_ and _**b** i_ . This procedure is known as simplified-SCCA (Parkhomenko et al., 2009; Witten et al., 2009), which enjoys a closed form solution.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: simplified SCCA/PMD-style formulations replace difficult covariance-induced constraints with simpler constraints on the weight vectors, making alternating optimization much cheaper. That is a fair high-level point. However, the statement that simplified-SCCA 'enjoys a closed form solution' is stronger than what is standard for these methods. In Witten et al.-style sparse CCA, each block update can often be expressed via soft-thresholding/normalization when the other vector is fixed, but the joint sparse problem is still nonconvex or bi-convex and is normally solved iteratively rather than by a single global closed-form solution analogous to classical CCA. If the authors mean 'closed-form coordinate/block updates,' that should be stated; otherwise this overstates the computational simplification.

comments.title: Abstract overstates the scope of the empirical comparison
comments.quote: Most notably, the method outperforms other linear and non-linear CCA models.
comments.explanation: The paper does evaluate DG-CCA on several synthetic and real examples and compares against multiple CCA-family baselines, so the authors have a reasonable basis for claiming improved empirical performance in their experiments. The wording, however, reads as a broad claim over 'other linear and non-linear CCA models' in general. From the provided context, the experiments cover selected baselines and datasets, not an exhaustive comparison against all relevant linear, kernel, regularized, and deep CCA variants under all regimes. A more defensible phrasing would be that the method outperforms the tested linear and nonlinear CCA baselines on the reported benchmarks.

comments.title: Total-correlation expression appears to omit the matrix square root / trace norm
comments.quote: The total correlation in Eq. 4 can be expressed using the _−_ 1 _/_ 2  _−_ 1 _−_ 1 _/_ 2 trace of **Σ**[] _Y_ **Σ** _Y X_  **Σ** _X_ **[Σ]**[] _[XY]_ **[Σ]**[] _Y_ .
comments.explanation: I initially tried to read through the OCR corruption here by matching it to the standard DCCA derivation. The intended covariance definitions just before this sentence are standard: \(\hat\Sigma_{XY}\), \(\hat\Sigma_X+\gamma I\), and \(\hat\Sigma_Y+\gamma I\). In DCCA, however, the total correlation is the sum of singular values of \(T=\hat\Sigma_X^{-1/2}\hat\Sigma_{XY}\hat\Sigma_Y^{-1/2}\), equivalently \(\operatorname{tr}((T^\top T)^{1/2})\). If the paper literally uses the trace of \(\hat\Sigma_Y^{-1/2}\hat\Sigma_{YX}\hat\Sigma_X^{-1}\hat\Sigma_{XY}\hat\Sigma_Y^{-1/2}\) without the outer matrix square root, then it is optimizing the sum of squared canonical correlations, not the usual total correlation used by DCCA. The surrounding prose says they are following DCCA and maximizing total correlation, so this may be a typesetting/OCR loss of the square-root notation, but as written the mathematical expression is not equivalent to the stated objective.

comments.title: Post-training gate selection rule is underspecified because the gates are random variables
comments.quote: After training we remove the stochastic part of the gates, and use only variables _ix ∈{_ 1 _, ..., DX }_ and _iy ∈{_ 1 _, ..., Dy}_ such that z _x_ [ _ix_ ] _>_ 0 and z _y_ [ _iy_ ] _>_ 0.
comments.explanation: The intended idea is clear and reasonable: train with stochastic relaxed gates, then form a deterministic selected feature set. What remains ambiguous is what exactly \(z\) denotes after the stochastic part is removed. Earlier \(z_i\) is a random clipped Gaussian variable, while the regularizer uses \(\mathbb E\|z\|_0\). If the intended deterministic gate is \(\max(0,\min(1,\mu_i))\), then the criterion is effectively \(\mu_i>0\); if it is based on the expected gate value or on \(P(z_i>0)\), the selected support could differ. This matters for reproducing the reported number of active gates and for interpreting feature selection. The issue is fixable by explicitly stating the deterministic test-time gate, e.g. \(z_i=\mathrm{clip}(\mu_i,0,1)\), and the threshold used.

comments.title: How DG-CCA produces an estimated canonical vector is not defined
comments.quote: Next, we present the values of _**φ**_ , the DG-CCA estimate (using _λ_ = 30) of the canonical vector _**φ**_ **[ˆ]** , and the CCA based estimate of the canonical vector _**a**_ **ˆ** .
comments.explanation: The synthetic experiment is meant to show that DG-CCA recovers the sparse support and improves canonical-vector estimation, which is a useful evaluation. But DG-CCA, as defined in the preceding model section, learns stochastic input gates and neural-network transformations; it does not directly define canonical vectors \(\hat\phi\) and \(\hat\eta\) in the same way linear CCA or SCCA does. If the networks are linear and \(\hat\phi\) is obtained from the product of the deterministic gates and the first-layer/output weights, that should be stated. If the plotted vector is instead just the gate vector or selected support, then comparing it with the true canonical vector using \(E_\phi=2(1-|\phi^\top\hat\phi|)\) is not fully specified. This affects the interpretability and reproducibility of Table 1’s estimation-error comparison.

comments.title: Ordinary CCA baseline is not well-defined in the stated high-dimensional setting
comments.quote: Using this model we generate 400 samples and estimate the canonical vectors based on CCA and DG-CCA.
comments.explanation: The authors are deliberately testing the regime \(N \ll D_X,D_Y\), and here the model has 800 variables per view but only 400 samples. Earlier they correctly note that standard closed-form CCA breaks or becomes degenerate when the number of variables exceeds the number of samples. In that context, an ordinary CCA estimate is not unique unless some implementation choice is made, such as using a pseudoinverse, adding ridge regularization, truncating the covariance matrices, or relying on a specific software default. The paper’s qualitative point that naive CCA fails is plausible, but the plotted “CCA based estimate” depends on an unspecified convention. A reader cannot reproduce that baseline or know whether the failure shown is due to a particular numerical choice rather than the intrinsic degeneracy.

comments.title: The explanation for why \(\ell_1\) input regularization fails is overstated
comments.quote: However, a straight forward _ℓ_ 1 regularization of the input layer of a neural network does not work in practice because it makes the learning procedure unstable. This was observed in the supervised setting by Li et al. (2016); Feng & Simon (2017). This instability occurs because the objective is not differentiable everywhere.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ motivation: they want a differentiable, stochastic-gate alternative to simple sparsity penalties, and prior supervised feature-selection work did report practical difficulties with naive \(\ell_1\)-style neural feature selection. The remaining concern is the causal explanation. Non-differentiability of \(\ell_1\) at zero is not, by itself, usually considered a sufficient reason that optimization “does not work”; subgradient, proximal, and smooth-approximation methods are standard and widely used. The practical instability may instead be due to interactions among feature weights, scale invariances across network layers, shrinkage bias, or optimization dynamics. As written, the claim overstates a general mathematical obstacle and may make STG appear necessary for a reason that is not fully justified.

comments.title: Empirical correlation formula appears to omit Pearson normalization
comments.quote: **Figure 3:** Left: Regularization path of DG-CCA on data generated from the linear model. The left _y_ -axis (green) represents the sum of active gates (by expectation) after training. The right _y_ -axis represents the empirical correlation between the estimated representations, i.e. _ρ_ ˆ = _**φ**_ **[ˆ]** _T_ _**X** T_ _**Y η**_ **ˆ** , where _**φ**_ **ˆ** and _**η**_ **ˆ** are the estimated canonical vectors.
comments.explanation: The authors are clearly trying to report the sample correlation between the one-dimensional projected views, and in the population model with \(\Sigma_X=\Sigma_Y=I\) and unit-norm true vectors it is true that \(\phi^T\Sigma_{XY}\eta=\rho\). I also interpret the displayed \(X^T Y\) ordering as likely an OCR/notation artifact for something like \(X Y^T\), since that is the dimensionally plausible expression. Even with that charitable reading, however, the expression shown is a cross-covariance/inner product, not an empirical Pearson correlation, unless additional normalization constraints are imposed on \(\hat\phi^T X\) and \(\hat\eta^T Y\). The empirical correlation should include the sample scaling and division by the empirical standard deviations, e.g. \(\hat\phi^T X Y^T\hat\eta / \sqrt{(\hat\phi^T X X^T\hat\phi)(\hat\eta^T Y Y^T\hat\eta)}\), up to the common \(N-1\) factors. This distinction matters especially in the high-dimensional, overfitting regime being illustrated, where estimated directions need not be unit variance under the sample covariance. If the plotted quantity is actually the normalized correlation computed by the DCCA loss, the caption should state that rather than giving the unnormalized bilinear form.

comments.title: How DG-CCA yields an estimated canonical vector is underspecified
comments.quote: Using this model we generate 400 samples and estimate the canonical vectors based on CCA and DG-CCA.
comments.explanation: For ordinary linear CCA, an estimated canonical vector \(\hat a\) or \(\hat b\) is a standard object. For DG-CCA, though, the learned parameters are stochastic gates plus neural-network weights; the method primarily selects input variables and learns representations, not canonical vectors in the classical SCCA sense. I can infer that in this synthetic linear experiment the authors may be using a one-dimensional linear network, in which case a canonical-vector estimate could be recovered from the product of the deterministic gates and the linear weights, followed by normalization and sign alignment. But this recovery step is not stated in the passage, even though the subsequent support recovery plot and the error \(E_\phi\) depend on it. Different plausible choices—using the gate vector alone, using the first-layer weights, using gate-weight products, or refitting CCA on the selected variables—would give different estimation errors. The claim is likely fixable, but the mapping from a trained DG-CCA model to \(\hat\phi\) and \(\hat\eta\) needs to be made explicit for this comparison to be reproducible.

comments.title: Reported “correlation” of 1.99 should be identified as total correlation
comments.quote: DG-CCA converges to embedding with a correlation of 1 _._ 99 using 372 and 403 pixels from views _**X**_ and _**Y**_ .
comments.explanation: Given the stated embedding dimension \(d=2\), I believe the authors intend the DCCA objective’s total correlation, i.e. the sum of the two canonical correlations, whose maximum is 2. This is consistent with the earlier description of DCCA as maximizing total correlation. However, the text says simply “a correlation of 1.99,” which conflicts with the standard convention that an individual correlation coefficient lies in \([-1,1]\). A careful reader can probably resolve this from context, but the statement is still potentially misleading and should say “total correlation of 1.99” or “sum of canonical correlations equal to 1.99.”

comments.title: Evaluation phrase “stacked embedding of the two views” is ambiguous
comments.quote: To measure the class separation in the learned embedding, we apply _k_ -means clustering to the stacked embedding of the two views.
comments.explanation: The evaluation goal is clear: assess whether the learned unsupervised representations separate MNIST digit classes. However, “stacked embedding of the two views” can reasonably mean at least two different procedures: vertically stacking the two sets of view embeddings as \(2N\) embedded samples, or concatenating the paired view embeddings into one higher-dimensional representation per sample. Because the two noisy MNIST views are described as different digit instances from the same class, this choice is not merely cosmetic; it changes the number of points clustered, the feature dimension, and potentially the reported MI/KM accuracy. The subsequent LSVM description has a similar ambiguity about whether one trains on each view separately, on vertically pooled embeddings, or on concatenated paired embeddings. This may be specified in the appendix, but in the main experimental description the evaluation protocol is underspecified enough to impede reproduction.

comments.title: Seismic evaluation protocol lacks a clearly separated test set
comments.quote: We omit 20% of the data as a validation set. Then we train DG-CCA to embed the data in 3 dimensions using several values for _λ_ = _λx_ = _λy_ . Then, we use the model that attains maximal correlation on the validation set. In Table 2 we present the MI, _k_ -means and SVM accuracies computed based on DG-CCA embedding.
comments.explanation: The Noisy MNIST experiment earlier gives a clear train/validation/test split, so I first looked for an analogous protocol here. For the seismic data, however, the only held-out subset mentioned is a validation set, and that same validation set is used to choose the regularization parameter by maximal correlation. It is then unclear whether the MI, k-means accuracy, and especially SVM accuracy in Table 2 are reported on the training data, on the validation data used for model selection, or on a separate test split not described here. This matters because the SVM metric is supervised and can be sensitive to train/test splitting, and using the validation set both for hyperparameter selection and final reporting would bias the reported performance upward. The authors may have used a fair split in the appendix, but as written this passage leaves a key part of the evaluation protocol underspecified.

comments.title: Application of ordinary CCA to seismic data is not explained despite D>N
comments.quote: Each Sonogram _**z** ∈_ R[1157] with 89 time bins and 13 frequency bins.
comments.explanation: The table reports a CCA baseline for the seismic experiment, and I checked whether ordinary CCA is well-defined in this setting. The passage states that the seismic subset contains 537 explosions, while each view has 1157 sonogram features. This is the high-dimensional regime where the sample covariance matrices are singular; earlier in the paper the authors explicitly note that direct CCA can fail when the number of features exceeds the number of samples. Thus a reader needs to know whether the CCA baseline used ridge regularization, PCA preprocessing, pseudoinverses, truncation, or some other stabilization. Without that detail, the CCA row in Table 2 is hard to interpret and may not be comparable to DG-CCA or the other baselines.

comments.title: Noise-generation procedure for seismic sonograms is underspecified
comments.quote: We create the noisy seismic data by adding sonograms computed based on vehicle noise from[1] .
comments.explanation: The intent is clear: the authors make the seismic classification problem harder by contaminating explosion sonograms with vehicle-noise sonograms. However, the exact construction is important and is not specified here. In particular, it is unclear whether the vehicle noise is added in the raw waveform domain or after computing sonograms, how the noise segments are selected, whether the same or independent noise is added to the E and N channels, and what amplitude normalization or SNR is used. Since sonograms may be magnitude, power, or log-scaled representations, simply saying that sonograms are added is not enough to reproduce the experiment or judge how severe the corruption is. This is a localized but real reproducibility gap.

comments.title: Conclusion overstates the scope of the empirical comparison
comments.quote: Finally, we demonstrate that the method outperforms existing methods for linear and non-linear canonical correlation analysis.
comments.explanation: The reported results do show DG-CCA outperforming the listed baselines on the paper's selected synthetic, Noisy MNIST, and seismic experiments, which supports the authors' main empirical story. The concern is the breadth of the wording: 'outperforms existing methods for linear and non-linear canonical correlation analysis' sounds like a general claim over the class of existing CCA methods and datasets. The experiments compare against several relevant methods, but not all existing linear and nonlinear CCA variants, and the superiority is shown only under the reported protocols. A more precise claim would be that DG-CCA outperforms the evaluated baselines on these experiments.

comments.title: Initialization is described as using the CCA closed-form solution, but the stated procedure is an SVD of cross-covariance
comments.quote: we can utilize the closed form solution of the CCA problem to derive a smarter initialization procedure for the parameters of the gates. Specifically, given the empirical covariance matrix _**C** XY_ = _**[XY]**_ ( _N −_ 1) _**[T]**_[, we denote the thresholded covariance matrix by] _**[ S]**[XY]_[ ,]
comments.explanation: I first read the garbled formula for \(C_{XY}\) as an OCR issue and assumed the intended definition is the usual empirical cross-covariance \(XY^\top/(N-1)\). With that charitable reading, the remaining concern is not the formula itself but the claim that this uses the closed-form CCA solution. Standard CCA does not take the leading singular vectors of the raw cross-covariance matrix; it uses a whitened cross-covariance such as \(\Sigma_X^{-1/2}\Sigma_{XY}\Sigma_Y^{-1/2}\), or equivalently a generalized eigenproblem involving within-view covariance matrices. The procedure described here thresholds \(C_{XY}\) and then takes leading singular vectors of \(S_{XY}\), which is closer to a covariance-maximizing/PLS-type initialization or simplified sparse CCA heuristic. This may be a perfectly reasonable initialization heuristic, especially if the data are standardized or if the authors only need a rough warm start, but calling it the closed-form CCA solution is mathematically misleading outside special cases such as identity within-view covariance.

comments.title: Threshold parameter for gate initialization is not specified reproducibly
comments.quote: Where _δ_ is a threshold value selected based on the estimated sparsity of _**X**_ and _**Y**_ . Specifically, if we assume that _r_ percent of the values should be zeroed, then _δ_ is set to be the _r_ -th percentile of _|_ ( _**C** XY_ ) _|_ . Then we compute the leading singular vectors _**u**_ and _**v**_ of _**S** XY_ . We further threshold the absolute values of these vectors (using the same percentile used for _**S** XY_ .
comments.explanation: The intended idea is clear: use a thresholded cross-covariance matrix to bias the gates toward features that appear cross-correlated. However, the actual initialization depends on the assumed sparsity level \(r\), and the passage does not state how \(r\) is chosen in the experiments or how it is estimated from data. This matters because DG-CCA is a nonconvex neural optimization problem and the gate initialization can affect which sparse solution is reached. There is also a small ambiguity in applying the “same percentile” first to all entries of a \(D_x\times D_y\) matrix and then to the entries of the left and right singular vectors: it is not fully specified whether the percentile is recomputed separately for \(u\) and \(v\), whether the same numerical threshold is reused, or whether separate view-specific sparsity assumptions are allowed. The broad heuristic is understandable, but a reader could not reproduce this initialization without additional choices.

comments.title: Calling \(\mu=0.5\) a “fair Bernoulli” initialization is potentially misleading for the active-gate probability
comments.quote: Yamada et al. (2020) have initialize all values of _**µ**_ to 0 _._ 5, in this case, the gates approximate “fair” Bernoulli parameters.
comments.explanation: I can see the authors' intent: for the clipped Gaussian gate \(z=\max\{0,\min\{1,\mu+\epsilon\}\}\), setting \(\mu=0.5\) makes the distribution symmetric around 0.5, so the expected gate value is plausibly around 0.5 and it resembles a relaxed fair Bernoulli in that sense. But in this paper the sparsity penalty is explicitly \(\mathbb{E}\|z\|_0=\sum_i \Pr(z_i>0)=\sum_i\Phi(\mu_i/\sigma)\). With the stated common choice \(\sigma=0.5\), initializing \(\mu=0.5\) gives \(\Pr(z_i>0)=\Phi(1)\approx0.84\), not 0.5. Thus, if “fair Bernoulli parameters” is read as the probability of an active gate, it conflicts with the paper's own \(\ell_0\) interpretation. This is mostly a wording/interpretation issue, but clarifying whether “fair” refers to \(\mathbb{E}[z_i]\) rather than \(\Pr(z_i>0)\) would avoid confusion.
